---
title: "2.1.2 Data transmission"
subtitle: "Data Transmission P50-P52"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)

## Data Transmission Factors

### Three considerations when transmitting data

Data transmission can be over a short distance (e.g. computer to printer) or over longer distances (e.g. global network). Three factors need to be considered:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">The direction</span> of data transmission (can data transmit in one direction only, or in both directions?)
- <span class="mark">The method</span> of transmission (how many bits can be sent at the same time?)
- <span class="mark">How data will be synchronised</span> (how to make sure the received data is in the correct order?)
:::

These factors are usually considered by a <span class="term">communication protocol</span>.

## Serial vs Parallel Transmission

### Bit-by-bit vs multi-bit transmission

Data can be transmitted in two ways:

::: {.tight-list}
**Serial data transmission (串行):**
- Data is sent <span class="mark">ONE BIT AT A TIME</span> over a <span class="mark">SINGLE WIRE/CHANNEL</span>
- Bits are sent one after the other as a single stream
- Works well over <span class="mark">long distances</span>
- Data transmitted at a <span class="mark">slower rate</span> than parallel
- Data will arrive at its destination <span class="mark">fully synchronised</span> (in the correct order)
- Example: connecting a computer to a printer via a USB connection

**Parallel data transmission (并行):**
- <span class="mark">SEVERAL BITS OF DATA</span> (usually 1 byte) are sent down <span class="mark">SEVERAL CHANNELS/WIRES</span> all at the same time
- Each channel/wire transmits one bit
- <span class="mark">Faster</span> method of data transmission than serial
- Works well over <span class="mark">short distances</span> only
- Example: internal circuits in a computer (very short distances between components, high-speed essential)
:::

Note: Both serial and parallel data transmission can be simplex, half-duplex or full-duplex.

## Data Skewing

### Problem with parallel transmission over long distances

Over longer distances (for example, over 20 metres), parallel data can become <span class="term">skewed</span> (数据偏移):

::: {.tight-list}
- Data can arrive <span class="mark">unsynchronised</span> — bits arrive out of order
- The <span class="mark">longer the wire, the worse</span> this can become
- Serial transmission does not have this problem: data arrives <span class="mark">fully synchronised</span> because only one channel/wire is used
:::

## Table 2.1: Serial vs Parallel Comparison

### Textbook comparison of transmission methods

::: {.clean-table}
| Serial | Parallel |
|---|---|
| <span class="mark">less risk of external interference</span> than with parallel (due to fewer wires) | <span class="mark">faster rate</span> of data transmission than serial |
| more <span class="mark">reliable</span> transmission over longer distances | works well over <span class="mark">shorter distances</span> (e.g. internal pathways on computer circuit boards) |
| transmitted bits won't have the risk of being <span class="mark">skewed</span> (out of synchronisation) | since several wires used, bits can arrive <span class="mark">out of synchronisation (skewed)</span> |
| used if amount of data is <span class="mark">relatively small</span> since transmission rate is slower (e.g. USB) | preferred method when <span class="mark">speed is important</span> |
| used to send data over <span class="mark">long distances</span> (e.g. telephone lines) | if data is <span class="mark">time-sensitive</span>, parallel is most appropriate |
| <span class="mark">less expensive</span> than parallel due to fewer hardware requirements | parallel ports require <span class="mark">more hardware</span>, making them more expensive |
| — | <span class="mark">easier to program input/output operations</span> when parallel used |
:::

## Simplex, Half-duplex, and Full-duplex

### Communication directions

::: {.clean-table}
| Mode | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One-way only | Computer to printer, broadcast TV/radio |
| **Half-duplex** | Both ways, but not simultaneously | Walkie-talkies |
| **Full-duplex** | Both ways simultaneously | Broadband internet connection, telephone call |
:::

## Simplex Transmission

### One-way communication

<span class="term">Simplex</span> (单工) occurs when data can be sent in <span class="mark">ONE DIRECTION ONLY</span> (for example, from sender to receiver):

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Data flows in one direction only</span>
- An example would be <span class="mark">sending data from a computer to a printer</span>
- Receiver cannot send data back
:::

## Half-duplex Transmission

### Alternating two-way communication

<span class="term">Half-duplex</span> (半双工) occurs when data is sent in <span class="mark">BOTH DIRECTIONS but NOT AT THE SAME TIME</span>:

::: {.tight-list}
- Data can be sent from 'A' to 'B' and from 'B' to 'A' along the same transmission line
- <span class="mark">But they can't both be done at the same time</span>
- An example would be a <span class="mark">walkie-talkie</span> where a message can be sent in one direction only at a time; but messages can be both received and sent
:::

## Full-duplex Transmission

### Simultaneous two-way communication

<span class="term">Full-duplex</span> (全双工) occurs when data can be sent in <span class="mark">BOTH DIRECTIONS AT THE SAME TIME</span> (simultaneously):

::: {.tight-list}
- Data can be sent from 'A' to 'B' and from 'B' to 'A' along the same transmission line <span class="mark">simultaneously</span>
- An example would be a <span class="mark">broadband internet connection</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### Understand transmission modes

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">three factors: direction, method, synchronisation</span> (via communication protocol)
- <span class="mark">serial: one bit at a time, single wire</span>, works over long distances, no skewing
- <span class="mark">parallel: several bits, several wires</span>, faster but limited to short distances, risk of <span class="mark">skewed data</span> (bits arrive out of synchronisation over >20m)
- <span class="mark">simplex: ONE DIRECTION ONLY</span> (e.g. computer to printer)
- <span class="mark">half-duplex: BOTH DIRECTIONS but NOT AT THE SAME TIME</span> (e.g. walkie-talkies)
- <span class="mark">full-duplex: BOTH DIRECTIONS AT THE SAME TIME</span> (e.g. broadband internet)
- both serial and parallel can be simplex, half-duplex or full-duplex
:::

## Bridge

### Next: USB (Universal Serial Bus)

The next deck covers USB connections and device communication protocols.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)
